fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the AWS lambda adapter thing we've been talking about. The basic idea is that devs should be able to take their existing fastify app and just... plug it into lambda without rewriting anything. The gateway sends us these weird event blobs and we need to turn them into something fastify understands, run the request through the app, and then package up the response in whatever shape lambda expects back.

There are a bunch of annoying edge cases around how the path gets computed — remember how we handled that stage-prefix stripping thing in the routing module? Same kind of logic applies here. Also the query string situation is a mess because different event versions encode things differently, and there's that comma thing for v2 payloads we discussed.

On the response side, cookies behave differently depending on which payload version we're dealing with, and we need to handle binary payloads correctly — the caller should be able to plug in their own logic for deciding when to base64 encode, not just rely on a static list.

Also important: the handler needs to work both as a promise and with the old callback style. And if something blows up internally, we absolutely cannot let a raw exception bubble up to the caller — it has to degrade gracefully. Oh and multipart uploads need to work too. Ping me if anything's unclear.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. On the request body side, if the event shows `isBase64Encoded: true`, we need to decode that body from Base64 to its raw bytes before handing it to the framework. And if the gateway didn’t give us a `content-length` header, we should compute it from the decoded byte length and inject it so the body parser sees the right raw bytes and length.

For path and method picking, the order matters. If `pathParameterUsedAsPath` is configured and that named path parameter exists on the event, we use that value prefixed with `/`. If not, we prefer the explicit `path` field on the event, and if that’s not there we fall back to `rawPath`. Method comes from `httpMethod` when it exists, otherwise from `requestContext.http.method`. Also on the stage-prefix thing, default behavior is still to strip the leading stage segment when `requestContext.stage` prefixes the `path` but the resource path does not start with that segment. If `options.retainStage: true` is set, we do not strip it and just leave the path alone. Same rule applies in the core path resolution bit as discussed.

On invocation style, the returned handler needs to work the same whether someone is awaiting it as a promise or calling it the old node way where the third argument is a function. Both modes should produce identical result shapes. If `options.callbackWaitsForEmptyEventLoop` is defined, write that boolean onto the invocation context object before request processing begins. And if something unexpected blows up internally during request processing, we still do not leak the raw exception. We return, or pass to the callback, a well-formed error result with `status: 500` and an empty body, with no response headers, in both promise and callback modes.

A few response details too. Non-string response header values need to be coerced to strings in the result. If the handler explicitly removes the Content-Type header, it should stay absent in the result and not get defaulted back in. And if the handler response body is empty, the returned body should be an empty string with a `content-length` of `0`.

For multipart, when a multipart/form-data parser is registered, uploaded file parts need to come through with `fieldname`, `filename`, `encoding`, `mimetype`, and `content`, where `content` is the file content as a string. Text fields should come through with `fieldname`, `encoding`, `mimetype`, and `value`. We need this to work for both plain raw string multipart bodies and Base64-encoded multipart bodies.

One other thing the team asked about: every incoming request should be decorated with `awsLambdaEvent` and `awsLambdaContext` directly on the request object, and that needs to be available not just in route handlers but also in lifecycle hooks like `onRequest`. There’s also an optional `decorateRequest` option if someone wants to customize that behavior a bit further.

And on binary responses, the custom hook for the encoding decision is `enforceBase64`, which is a caller-supplied predicate function `(headers) => boolean` checked during response encoding selection. That’s the extension point we want people using instead of baking in more fixed rules.

trigger_keywords: multi-valued query parameters
how are repeated query params handled
multiValueQueryStringParameters behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the event provides `multiValueQueryStringParameters`, those values take precedence and each parameter is exposed to the handler as an array of its values. For payload format version 2.0, a single query value containing commas is split into an array by default; this behavior can be disabled by setting `options.parseCommaSeparatedQueryParams: false`. Splitting must not mutate the original event object.

trigger_keywords: ELB query string URL decoding
load balancer event query params
should query keys and values be URL decoded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For load-balancer sourced events (identified by `requestContext.elb` being present), both query parameter keys and values must be URL-decoded before being forwarded to the framework. For all other event sources (API Gateway v1, v2), query values are passed through verbatim without any URL-decoding.

trigger_keywords: cookies in v2 event
how are cookies forwarded from the event to the handler
protocol v2 cookie array handling
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For payload format v2.0, the `cookies` array in the event must be joined into a single `Cookie` request header (semicolon or comma joined) and forwarded to the framework so the handler sees it as a normal Cookie header.

trigger_keywords: multi-value request headers promotion
when are multi-value headers merged into one
multiValueHeaders promotion rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A header from `multiValueHeaders` is only promoted to the request if it carries more than one value. A multi-header entry with exactly one value is ignored (not promoted). When a header has multiple values, they are comma-joined into a single combined header value and forwarded to the framework.

trigger_keywords: serializing the lambda event into a header
x-apigateway-event header
serializeLambdaArguments option
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `options.serializeLambdaArguments: true` is set, the library URL-encodes the original invocation event as JSON and injects it into the request as the `x-apigateway-event` header. This header is absent by default (when the option is not set or is false).

trigger_keywords: Set-Cookie response header differences between v1 and v2
how cookies are returned in the response
cookies field vs multiValueHeaders in response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For protocol v2.0 responses, Set-Cookie headers must be placed in a dedicated top-level `cookies` array in the result, not in the headers map. For v1 (no version field) responses, Set-Cookie values must be placed under `multiValueHeaders` as an array. Other multi-valued response headers in v1 are comma-joined into a single `headers` entry.

trigger_keywords: when should the response body be base64 encoded
binary mime type matching
custom predicate for base64 encoding decision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The response body is Base64-encoded and `isBase64Encoded` set to true when: (1) the response Content-Type matches one of the configured `binaryMimeTypes`; (2) the response has a non-identity `Content-Encoding` header (e.g. `br`, `gzip`); or (3) a caller-supplied `enforceBase64` predicate function returns true given the response headers. The predicate is open for extension without modifying the core library.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that comma thing for v2 payloads
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `parseCommaSeparatedQueryParams` option (default true): for payload version 2.0 events, comma-separated query values are split into arrays; controlled by `options.parseCommaSeparatedQueryParams`, tested in `feature3_2_comma_separated_query.json`

AWS API Gateway proxy integration events in three shapes: payload format v1.0 (httpMethod + path), payload format v2.0 (version: '2.0' + rawPath + requestContext.http), and Application Load Balancer events (requestContext.elb present) — each requiring different normalization paths in the event parsing unit

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to look at the existing routing and encoding modules in the codebase to find the right pattern, or check with the original dev who built the login compatibility logic we referenced earlier.

feature10_1_multipart_file_fields.json.description: Parse a multipart/form-data body (provided either as a raw string or Base64-encoded) and expose the uploaded file part to the handler.

event.body:
------WebKitFormBoundaryDP6Z1qHQSzB6Pf8c
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="uploadFile1"; filename="test.txt"
Content-Type: text/plain

Hello World!
------WebKitFormBoundaryDP6Z1qHQSzB6Pf8c--

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"uploadFile1":{"fieldname":"uploadFile1","filename":"test.txt","encoding":"7bit","mimetype":"text/plain","content":"Hello World!"}}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=132
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"uploadFile1":{"fieldname":"uploadFile1","filename":"test.txt","encoding":"7bit","mimetype":"text/plain","content":"Hello World!"}}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=132
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

feature10_2_multipart_value_field.json.description: Parse a multipart/form-data text field and expose its decoded value to the handler.

cases.expected_output:
[response] status=200 isBase64Encoded=false body={"html":{"fieldname":"html","encoding":"7bit","mimetype":"text/plain","value":"

Hello World

"}} header.connection=keep-alive header.content-length=100 header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8 header.date=

feature1_request_body.json.description: Decode an incoming request body and forward it to the routed handler, computing the Content-Length header; supports both a plain UTF-8 body and a Base64-encoded body.

cases.expected_output:
[request]
request.headers.content-type=application/json
request.headers.content-length=14
request.body={"greet":"hi"}
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world2"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=18
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

[request]
request.headers.content-type=application/json
request.headers.content-length=15
request.body={"greet":"hi"}
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world2"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=18
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

feature2_1_path_resolution.json.description: Choose the path the framework routes to: prefer the explicit request path, and fall back to the raw path (deriving the method from the request context) when only that is present.

request.url=/test
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17

header.date=<present>

request.query.t=1698604776681
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

feature4_3_serialize_event_header.json.description: Optionally serialize the original invocation event into a URL-encoded request header so handlers can inspect the raw event; the header is absent when the feature is off (the default).

cases.expected_output:
[request]
request.headers.x-apigateway-event=%7B%22version%22%3A%222.0%22%2C%22httpMethod%22%3A%22GET%22%2C%22path%22%3A%22%2Ftest%22%2C%22headers%22%3A%7B%22X-My-Header%22%3A%22wuuusaaa%22%7D%7D
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

[request]
request.headers.x-apigateway-event=<undefined>
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

feature5_1_response_json_cookies_v2.json.description: Render a JSON handler response into a proxy result; for protocol v2, Set-Cookie headers are returned as a dedicated cookie list rather than response headers.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>
cookies=qwerty=one,qwerty=two

feature5_2_response_v1_cookies_and_joine.description: For protocol v1 (no version field), Set-Cookie values are returned under a multi-value headers map; other multi-valued response headers are comma-joined into a single header value.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world2"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=18
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>
header.x-custom-header=ciao,salve
multiValueHeaders.set-cookie=qwerty=one,qwerty=two

feature5_3_response_empty_and_coercion.j.description: An empty handler response yields an empty body with zero Content-Length; non-string response header values are coerced to strings; removing Content-Type omits it from the result.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body=
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=0
header.date=<present>
header.x-non-string=1717171717171
multiValueHeaders.set-cookie=qwerty=one,qwerty=two

feature6_1_binary_mime_types.json.description: When the response Content-Type matches a configured binary MIME type, the body is Base64-encoded and flagged as such.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=true
body=SGVsbG8gYmluYXJ5IHBheWxvYWQh
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=21
header.content-type=application/octet-stream
header.date=<present>

feature6_2_content_encoding_base64.json.description: A compressed response (non-identity Content-Encoding) is Base64-encoded by default even without a configured binary MIME type.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=true
body=SGVsbG8gYmluYXJ5IHBheWxvYWQh
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-encoding=br
header.content-length=21
header.content-type=application/octet-stream
header.date=<present>

feature6_3_custom_base64_predicate.json.description: A caller-supplied predicate decides whether a response should be Base64-encoded based on the response headers.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=true
body=SGVsbG8gYmluYXJ5IHBheWxvYWQh
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=21
header.content-type=application/octet-stream
header.date=<present>
header.x-base64-encoded=1

feature7_1_request_decoration.json.description: Decorate the request so the handler can read the original invocation event and the original invocation context.

cases.expected_output:
[request]
request.awsLambdaEvent.httpMethod=GET
request.awsLambdaContext={"functionName":"fn"}
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

feature7_2_decoration_in_hook.json.description: The request decoration is available in lifecycle hooks that run before the handler, not only inside the handler.

cases.expected_output:
[request]
onRequest.awsLambdaEvent.httpMethod=GET
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>

feature8_1_callback_invocation.json.description: Support node-style callback invocation: the proxy delivers the same proxy result through a callback as it does through a promise.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>
cookies=qwerty=one,qwerty=two

feature8_2_callback_waits_option.json.description: When provided, the empty-event-loop-wait flag is written onto the invocation context before the request is processed.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>
[context]
context.callbackWaitsForEmptyEventLoop=false

[response]
status=200
isBase64Encoded=false
body={"hello":"world"}
header.connection=keep-alive
header.content-length=17
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.date=<present>
[context]
context.callbackWaitsForEmptyEventLoop=true

feature9_error_resilience.json.description: If the underlying request processing fails unexpectedly, the proxy returns a 500 result with an empty body and no headers instead of leaking the failure, for both promise and callback invocation.

cases.expected_output:
[response]
status=500
isBase64Encoded=<undefined>
body=

[response]
status=500
isBase64Encoded=<undefined>
body=
